cards.definition: The combined ecological output of a shade-grown coffee system — carbon sequestration, biodiversity habitat, moisture retention, and microclimate regulation — running well beyond what the coffee crop itself provides.
cards.analogy: A roof that's simultaneously insulation, a rain catchment, and a wildlife shelter, not just cover.
cards.why_it_matters: The baseline your sourcing story is pricing a premium against — worth quantifying rather than treating as a vague marketing claim.
cards.cloze: ___ refers to the combined carbon storage, biodiversity habitat, and moisture regulation a shade-grown system provides beyond the crop itself.

cards.definition: Removing native shade trees to chase a near-term yield boost. It measurably reduces biodiversity, carbon stocks, and soil moisture retention, and on steep slopes accelerates erosion — a cost that shows up in later seasons, after the short-term gain has already been banked.
cards.analogy: Selling the roof to insulate the house for one winter — it works briefly, but every job the roof was doing stops at once.
cards.why_it_matters: A farmgate premium for keeping the canopy intact is, in effect, a premium for the farmer who didn't sell the roof.
cards.cloze: ___ trades long-term soil, carbon, and moisture health for a near-term yield boost, with the cost showing up in later seasons.
